blocks.html: High class consonants are inherently RISING tone; they have a rising tone by default. When pronouncing their names, you have to pronounce them with a rising tone like ŗłāŗł≠ [kŇŹr] ŗłĖŗł≠ [tŇŹr] ŗłČŗł≠ [chŇŹr] etc.

You can see that the sounds of the high class consonants are IDENTICAL to those of the LC2 consonants, but the HC counterparts come with a default rising tone. Their final sounds are also identical. LC2
ŗłģ HC
ŗłČ*
ŗłú*
ŗłĚ*

ŗłę Initial k t ch s p f h Final 17 -k -t -p Final consonants (continued) At this point, we have learnt all the common consonants and their pronunciations at both the initial and final position. We can summarise what certain consonants would sound like at final position: Initial ng m n r l y w Final -ng -m -n -i -o Initial k t ch s p f h g j d dt b bp (-) Final -k -t -p Again, there‚Äôs no need to memorise this table. All you need to do is ‚Äúun- release‚ÄĚ the final consonant sound.
/chim/ /goon/ /s√†yk/

See how LC2 and HC have the same consonant sounds, but note that they have completely different tone patterns for both live and dead syllables. We will discuss the rules that govern this in the next section. Some of the words don‚Äôt have any meaning but are used as contrastive examples. Please only focus on the pronunciation.

ŗłęŗł∂ŗłá-ŗłģŗł∂ŗłá Low born, but high class High class consonant ‚Äúŗłę‚ÄĚ has a very strange habit‚ÄĒit changes low class consonants to high class! When you put ŗłę in front of a low class 1 consonant, ŗłę becomes silent, and that LC1 consonant changes to high class; meaning the HC tone rule is applied instead of the LC tone rule (We‚Äôll learn about it tomorrow). First, let‚Äôs look at these examples below and you‚Äôll get what I‚Äôm talking about:
/naam/ /n√≠/ /n√ļk/ /n√īrk/ Just a low class consonant that acts in a low class way, right?
ŗłęŗłôŗłī*

/nńÉam/ /n√¨/ /n√Ļk/ /n√≤rk/ Now, the tone in each word changes, doesn‚Äôt it? At this point I want you to be aware of it, we‚Äôll dive deeper into the rules on DAY 5. Keep in mind, however, that ŗłę can only do this to ‚Äúlow class 1‚ÄĚ consonants. You can even consider them as separate consonants in high class if it makes it easier for you. Just like this:

blocks.thai: ŗłęŗłá- ŗłęŗł°- ŗłęŗłô- ŗłęŗł£- ŗłęŗł•- ŗłęŗłĘ- ŗłęŗłß-

blocks.html: [mňė] [nňė] [rňė] [lňė] [yňė] [wňė] Now you have a complete set of identical consonant sounds in both high class and low class! HC
ŗłę LC
In this exercise notice that the first ŗłę is silent, and the following LC1 consonant becomes an HC consonant. Don‚Äôt worry about the tones.
21 ŗłęŗłôŗł≤ŗł°

ŗłęŗł•ŗł≤ŗłĘŗłęŗł°ŗł≠ Somewhere I belong So far you have learnt 28 consonants in total. Now, you might have a little problem memorising which class each consonant belongs to. If you find it a bit difficult then I might be able to assist with some memory aids I‚Äôve created for you. The key is ‚Äúthe sounds‚ÄĚ. Apart from HC and LC2, if you know the sound of the consonant you‚Äôll know which class it belongs to. HC
[tňė] [chňė] [sňė] [pňė] [fňė] [hňė] MC
[t] [ch] [s] [p] [f] [h] So I‚Äôve created mnemonics for you!

blocks.thai: MID class: ŗłĀ ŗłĒ ŗłē ŗłą ŗłö ŗłõ ŗł≠
blocks.translit: ‚ÄúG O O D J O B !‚ÄĚ

blocks.html: The letters ‚Äėg‚Äô ‚Äėd‚Äô ‚Äėj‚Äô and ‚Äėb‚Äô represent the consonant sounds in mid class, ‚Äėd‚Äô represents both ŗłĒ [d] and ŗłē [dt], and ‚Äėb‚Äô represents both ŗłö [b] and ŗłõ [bp]. However, since ŗł≠ has no sound you‚Äôll just have to remember that it‚Äôs mid class, perhaps by associating it with that exclamation mark!

blocks.thai: LOW class 1: ŗłĘ ŗłß ŗłô ŗłá ŗł• ŗł° ŗł£
blocks.translit: ‚ÄúY A W N I N G L E M U R‚ÄĚ

blocks.html: The letters ‚Äėy‚Äô ‚Äėw‚Äô ‚Äėn‚Äô ‚Äėng‚Äô ‚Äėl‚Äô ‚Äėm‚Äô and ‚Äėr‚Äô represent the consonant sounds in low class 1. And since lemurs are animals and ‚Äúlower‚ÄĚ than humans, they‚Äôre LOW class.

blocks.thai: LOW class 2: ŗłĄ ŗłó ŗłä ŗłě ŗłü ŗłč ŗłģ
blocks.translit: ‚ÄúK E T C H U P F I S H‚ÄĚ

blocks.html: The letters ‚Äėk‚Äô ‚Äėt‚Äô ‚Äėch‚Äô ‚Äėp‚Äô ‚Äėf‚Äô ‚Äės‚Äô and ‚Äėh‚Äô (remember ‚Äės‚Äô and ‚Äėh‚Äô separately because in Thai there‚Äôs no ‚Äėsh‚Äô) represent the consonant sounds in low class 2. And since fish are animals and ‚Äúlower‚ÄĚ than humans, they‚Äôre LOW class. But HC consonants, alas, have the same sounds as LC2, so you‚Äôll just have to memorise these consonants in your own way. So if you come across a ‚ÄúKETCHUP FISH‚ÄĚ consonant and it‚Äôs not one of the HC consonants (plus 3 more consonants that we‚Äôll learn in the future), then you know immediately that it‚Äôs an LC2 consonant:
[tňė] [chňė] [sňė] [pňė] [fňė] [hňė]

The final exercise today is slightly different: we‚Äôre not reading words, but we‚Äôre going to place these consonants into the right classes. Use the mnemonics I introduced you to and start categorising away!
ŗłģ HIGH CLASS MID CLASS LOW CLASS 1 LOW CLASS 2 (Answer) 22

items: High class consonants have a rising tone by default
High class consonants can pair with low class 2; their sounds are identical, both at initial and final position
When ŗłę precedes a low class 1 consonant, ŗłę becomes silent and that LC1 consonant changes its class to high class

Now you should be able to put all these consonants into the right classes. If you still can‚Äôt, only memorise HC consonants, then drill these mnemonics: ‚ÄúGood job!‚ÄĚ - MC; ‚ÄúYawning lemur‚ÄĚ - LC1; ‚ÄúKetchup fish‚ÄĚ - LC2 Finally, our first glimpse of how the Thai tone system works! This chapter‚Äôs content can be a bit overwhelming so read carefully and make sure you understand it before progressing further. Today we‚Äôve got more vowels with two forms as well.
